caption: Figure 1. WBDF TEM micrograph of a dissociated dislocation in the base alloy. The annotations are explained in the text.
paper_abstract: Figure 3(a) shows the measurements of partial dislocation spacing as a function of dislocation angle for the base, 2.5 Si and 2.5 Al alloys. In Figure 3(a), the solid lines represent the average calculated SFE for each material and the dashed lines indicate the upper and lower bounds from the calculated SFE standard deviations. The base alloy SFE is 26.3 mJ ${ \mathrm { m } } ^ { - 2 } ,$ which is consistent with the value of 30 mJ $\mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 }$ found for a similar 15 Ni–11 Cr alloy through TEM analysis of extended dislocation nodes [25]. Figure 3(b) shows the dependence of SFE on relative Si and Al alloying. Figure 3(b) indicates that 2.5 wt.% Al alloying increases the SFE by 12.0 mJ m2 from the base alloy value whereas 2.5 wt.% Si alloying decreases it by 6.4 mJ $\dot { \mathrm { m } } ^ { - 2 }$ . A correction is available to account for the effect of the partial dislocation strain fields on the observed partial dislocation spacings and thus the measured SFE values [34]. The correction suggested by Williams and Carter [34] was not applied because not all of the TEM data was available to assign all of the necessary parameters. An upper limit estimation suggests that the biggest change is in the 2.5 Al alloy, where the correction would increase the SFE of the 2.5 Al alloy to $4 4 . 0 \pm 9 . 8 \mathrm { m J } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 }$ . The SFEs of the other alloys would change by approximately 1 mJ $\mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 }$ or less. Since the corrected values are estimated, the uncorrected data will be used for the remainder of the paper unless otherwise noted.
paper_title: The influence of silicon and aluminum alloying on the lattice parameter and stacking fault energy of austenitic steel
citation_sentences: For each material, multiple data pairs of partial dislocation separation, $d ,$ and perfect dislocation character angle, $\alpha ,$ were recorded along several dislocations. Figure 1 shows an example WBDF image of a dissociated dislocation in the base alloy, along with representations of d and a. As shown in Figure 1, a is the angle between the line vector and the Burgers vector, b, of the undissociated dislocation. Because both partial dislocations are in contrast in the condition shown and because g. $\mathbf { \delta } . \mathbf { b _ { p } }$ analysis was performed, it is known that the g vector used to form the two-beam condition is parallel to the undissociated dislocation Burgers vector, b. The paired (d, a) data were then input into Eq. (1) [35] to calculate the SFE values for each measured data pair:

caption: Figure 3. (a) Dependence of partial dislocation separation on perfect dislocation character angle for the base, 2.5 Si and 2.5 Al alloys. The symbols indicate the measured data while the solid lines indicate the calculated trends based on the calculated average SFE for each material. The dashed lines indicate the upper and lower bounds determined from the calculated SFE standard deviation for each material. (b) Dependence of SFE on Si and Al alloying.
